summary: I was born in Sacramento, near the present Coca-Cola plant on Stockton Boulevard, on August 12, 1910. My family lived in Sacramento City after moving from Broderick and later I attended Sacramento High School, which was about ten miles from where we lived. Sacramento was home to a diverse community including Japanese, Italian, and Portuguese families during my youth. I also worked and lived in North Sacramento after high school, where there was a significant Japanese presence.

I was born just behind what is now the Coca-Cola plant on Stockton Boulevard in Sacramento. At the time of my birth in 1910, the Coca-Cola plant was not yet established, but that location is a landmark I often mention to describe where I grew up. It helps place my early childhood in the context of Sacramento's development.

My father worked for Gerber Products Company while we lived near Sacramento. During that time, he was also president of the California National Bank and owned a large estate that resembled a cattle ranch. This was around the early 1910s before we moved to Broderick when I was about two years old.

My father was president of the California National Bank when we lived near Sacramento. I remember going to the bank at 4th and J Street, which was the California National Bank, and getting scolded for bringing loose pennies instead of wrapped rolls. It was a memorable experience from my childhood, showing how careful you had to be with money even then.

After leaving Sacramento, my family moved to Broderick in Yolo County, where we farmed a five-acre truck farm growing vegetables like peppers, eggplants, and cucumbers. I attended a one-room Japanese language school there, which met for about an hour after regular school five or six days a week. We lived in Broderick for about six years before moving back to Sacramento City.

Broderick, where my family farmed for several years, is located in Yolo County. Our farm was a small truck farm where we grew various vegetables. This rural setting was quite different from Sacramento City, and it was where I attended grammar school and the Japanese language school.

Around 1924 or 1925, when I was about 14 or 15, my family moved to Riverside, California, to a 15-acre farm on Riverside Boulevard. My father was older by then and relied on me and my cousin to do the heavy farm work, using horses instead of tractors. I attended high school in Sacramento while living in Riverside, commuting about ten miles by passenger car.

I attended grammar school near Sutterville Road, close to our farm in Riverside. The school was called Sutterville Heights, but the building is now gone and replaced by residential development. Also, the Japanese language school near Sutterville Road was located just south of it off Riverside Boulevard, close to where Portuguese Hall now stands.

The Japanese language school in Riverside was located near where the present Portuguese Hall is now. This school was torn down during wartime. I never attended that school, but I remember its location well from my time living in Riverside in the 1920s and 1930s.

I attended Sacramento High School while living in Riverside, commuting about ten miles by passenger car. I graduated from there before continuing to farm for a few more years with my cousin. Sacramento High was an important part of my youth and education during the mid-1920s.

My parents were both from Hiroshima, Japan. I visited Japan for the first time in 1931, staying near friends of my father who had farmed with him in Broderick. Sadly, during my later visit about fifty years ago, I learned that the atomic bomb had devastated Hiroshima, and the family of my childhood friends had suffered great losses, including the death of their parents and the loss of her husband in the Philippine campaign during World War II.

My mother came to America through Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada, around 1906 or 1907. She brought with her an adopted son when she immigrated. Many Japanese immigrants at that time came through Vancouver, and my family was part of that migration route.

Sumitomo Bank came into the Sacramento area after World War II. I recall that there was a Japanese bank at 4th and L Street, which might have been either Sumitomo Bank or Yokohama Specie Bank, but I am not certain. Before the war, Japanese banks were limited, and the Bank of America and Bank of Italy were more common in the area.

Yokohama Specie Bank was one of the Japanese banks in Sacramento, possibly located at 4th and L Street, similar to Sumitomo Bank. I am not sure which bank it was exactly, but it was the only Japanese bank in the area before or after the war. The Japanese community had limited banking options compared to larger banks like Bank of America.

Bank of America was one of the main banks in Sacramento during my youth. It was more established than the Japanese banks at the time. I remember that the Bank of Italy was also present, but Bank of America was a key financial institution in the area where I lived and worked.

The Bank of Italy was a prominent bank in Sacramento when I was growing up. It was one of the banks that the local community, including Japanese residents, would have known. Later, the Bank of Italy became part of what is now Bank of America.

Southland Park was located on Riverside Boulevard near the end of the streetcar line. There used to be a swimming pool there, but it was off limits to Japanese people like me when I lived in Riverside in the 1920s and 1930s. The Jewish Temple now stands where the pool used to be, but back then, Japanese were not allowed to use the pool even though there were no signs posted.

I attended a Japanese language school in Broderick, which was a one-room schoolhouse where we learned Japanese for about an hour after regular school, five or six days a week. The teacher was named Takahashi, and the school had about 15 students. Later, in Riverside, there was a small Japanese language school near Sutterville Road, but I never attended it.
cues: five or six days a week

entity: Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki
summary: The atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki had a profound impact on my family and friends. When I visited Japan about fifty years after my first trip, I learned that the family of my childhood friends in Hiroshima had lost their parents in the bombing. Both girls survived, but the devastation was immense and changed many lives, including those connected to my family.

During World War II, the husband of one of my childhood friends from Hiroshima died in the Philippine campaign. This was a tragic loss for the family, who had already suffered from the atomic bombing. The war touched many lives connected to my family and friends both in Japan and America.

Although not explicitly detailed in my story, the Japanese American internment during World War II affected the community I was part of. For example, the Japanese language school near Portuguese Hall was torn down during wartime, likely related to the internment period. The war disrupted many aspects of Japanese American life in Sacramento and Riverside.
